data.title: Provident Financial Services: 3 Reasons Why I Wouldn't Buy This Bank
data.description:
Provident Financial Services (NYSE:PFS) is a bank founded way back in 1839 and based in Jersey City, New Jersey. Its dividend yield is currently quite high at 5.39%, and investors are wondering how attractive it really is.

As we will see in this article, the sustainability of the dividend does not seem to be a problem today, but my rating is still not a buy. Personally, I do not see the potential for a satisfactory capital gain, and above all, there are three reasons that lead me to avoid it.

The three reasons that lead me to avoid it

Before discussing the three reasons that lead me to avoid it, I would like to point out that PFS is a solid bank overall, and my intention is not to cause alarm. There are simply certain characteristics that lead me to avoid it as an investment, but that does not mean that it does not have positive aspects.

For example, the net interest margin is steadily rising, a sign that the company is navigating well in a rather complex macroeconomic environment. Interest rates have remained unchanged, but expectations about rates are constantly changing, yet PFS has managed to improve its profitability.

At the same time, its tangible book value per share, the most important component in a bank's valuation, continues to rise gradually. Clearly, unrealized losses on AFS securities are still weighing heavily, but the Fed seems determined to cut rates in September, which may give bond yields some breathing room.

That said, here are the three factors that lead me to avoid this bank.

CRE concentration ratio off the norm

The CRE concentration ratio is composed of CRE loans in the numerator and total capital in the denominator; the higher it is, the more the bank is exposed to CRE loans. As the latter are rather cyclical (and therefore risky), supervisory authorities suggest keeping it below 300%, but banks can go further if they wish. Well, PFS has gone further, much further.

It currently stands at 443%, a level that I consider far too risky. Although there have been improvements compared to last year, we are still a long way from 300%, which is already very high. This means that the bank is heavily exposed to CRE loans and could be hit hard in the event of a recession.

Personally, I prefer banks that are less cyclical than average, and PFS is the exact opposite. Clearly, the reverse is also true. When the economy is strong, cyclical businesses can deliver exciting performances, but doubts arise after the recent labor market report revision. The Fed will almost certainly cut rates in September as the labor market is creaking, and this does not seem to me to be the ideal macro scenario for such a cyclical bank.

Loan-to-deposit ratio too high

Personally, I prefer banks with a loan-to-deposit ratio below 90%, as this gives them room for maneuver should they want to take advantage of certain opportunities. PFS's loan-to-deposit ratio is 102%, meaning that loans exceed total deposits.

As a result, its balance sheet appears rather rigid and not very receptive to change. With not enough deposits and being fully invested, it is difficult to further increase the loan portfolio.

Compared to the previous quarter, there was an increase of 1.70%, a rather low growth rate that is unlikely to reach mid-single digits: the deposit base is not large enough to finance origination. A bank with an inflexible balance sheet is bound to break when times get tough.

Furthermore, still focusing on assets, non-performing loans are gradually increasing in relation to total loans. This is not an alarming situation, but it is not positive either. Basically, loans are growing slowly, and those already on the balance sheet are showing signs of weakness.

Deposit composition is deteriorating

The bank's rigidity stems from deposits that are struggling to improve.

The results for the last quarter were not encouraging, as the composition of deposits took a step backwards. Not only did non-interest bearing deposits fall by 0.60%, but wholesale deposits (including brokered deposits) rose by 80.40%. The latter are rather expensive and point to PFS's inability to raise capital at low cost.

If the bank were able to attract more depositors, the benefits would also be reflected in the loan-to-deposit ratio. Deposits are the lifeblood of banks, and without them they are stuck.

Conclusion

Despite these three aspects, PFS remains a solid bank, but it must improve its deposits. With them it could finance new loans, increase its net interest income, and ultimately, its tangible book value.

The bank currently offers a very attractive dividend yield of 5.39%, which seems quite sustainable

EPS covers it amply, but take a look at dividend growth: the 5Y CAGR was 0.85%. In other words, the dividend is sustainable because it has remained unchanged. If it had increased, EPS would have barely covered it.

If you invest in this bank, you could get your 5.39% gross return over the next few years, but personally, I avoid dividend-paying companies if the dividend is not increased over time. For me, the dividend yield on cost is the most important thing, and I doubt that PFS can guarantee that.

Finally, the stock does not even look cheap at the moment.

Its price to tangible book value is almost perfectly in line with the 3Y average, which is why I believe the stock is fairly valued. I don't see much upside at the current price, just a high but flat dividend. However, is it really worth it?

The surprising labor market data has cast doubt on the true strength of the US economy, and at this stage I would avoid overly cyclical businesses regardless: PFS is definitely one of these and does not even appear to be trading at a discount.

data.title: Differentiation Matters As Moat Stocks Lead In July
data.description:
In a market dominated by mega-cap momentum, July showcased the strength of the Moat Index’s stock selection and structural discipline.

In July, U.S. equity markets continued their summer advance, marking the third straight month of gains as the S&P 500 rose 2.2% and set a fresh new record high. The Nasdaq Composite gained 3.7%, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average ended the month largely flat. Investor sentiment was bolstered by strong corporate earnings, positive GDP data, and the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill, which enacted new tax legislation extending previous cuts. However, renewed tariff discussions with multiple countries and a softer-than-expected jobs report toward month's end introduced volatility. The Technology sector remained at the forefront, propelled by AI developments, even as the Federal Reserve once again kept interest rates unchanged. Continued large downward revisions in jobs data did, however, boost market expectations for possible rate reductions later in the year.

The Morningstar Wide Moat Focus Index (the “Moat Index”) gained more than 3% in July, outpacing both the broad market S&P 500 as well as the equal-weighted variant. The outperformance came despite the Moat Index’s structural underweight to top mega-cap technology names. Strong stock selection during the month, particularly within the consumer staples and industrial sectors, more than offset sector allocations. The strategy continues to provide differentiated exposure, which has become increasingly difficult for investors to find amid historical levels of concentration in the U.S. equity markets.

Smaller stocks also saw advances during the month, but like much of the year so far, the cohort’s performance lagged relative to large caps. The Morningstar US Small-Mid Cap Moat Focus Index (the “SMID Moat Index”) posted a 1.9% gain in July, outpacing both the broad small- and mid-cap benchmarks which returned 0.9% and 1.6%, respectively. Despite their muted performance, many are keeping a close eye on small and mid caps as the segment’s prospects could improve with a dovish pivot by the Fed.

Moat Stocks Lead the Pack in July

Moat Index July Highlights: Chips and Ships Steer Gains

In July, the Moat Index benefited from strong stock selection, as several companies delivered solid earnings results that prompted the market to revalue their shares more in line with Morningstar’s fair value estimates. This earnings-driven rerating more than offset headwinds from the Index’s equal-weighted construction in the current mega-cap driven market. As a result, the Index outpaced the broader, tech-heavy market while continuing to provide differentiated exposure.

Wide-moat semiconductor testing leader Teradyne (TER) was the top contributor to the Moat Index in July, with shares rising nearly 20% following second-quarter earnings and positive forward commentary. While sales declined year over year, management’s guidance pointed to a return to growth in the second half of 2025, and upbeat messaging around demand for AI chip testing and robotics drove a sharp rerating. Morningstar believes Teradyne is well positioned to benefit from growing complexity in semiconductors, particularly in high-bandwidth memory and custom AI accelerators, and sees a recent robotics design win as a meaningful growth driver heading into 2026. With its leading market share, sticky customer relationships, and a robust R&D pipeline, Morningstar maintains a $115 fair value estimate, suggesting there may still be additional upside ahead.

Defense contractor Huntington Ingalls (HII) was also a key contributor to Moat Index performance in July, with shares gaining on the back of strong quarterly results and guidance. While quarterly profit declined due to temporary shipbuilding delays, revenue rose modestly, and management highlighted the arrival of key components that should allow construction to accelerate in the second half of the year. Morningstar expects gradual margin improvement over the coming years, driven by more efficient throughput and higher-margin contracts, particularly in submarine programs. With the U.S. Navy continuing to invest in shipyard capacity and submarine production, Morningstar sees durable long-term demand for Huntington Ingalls’ uniquely positioned shipbuilding operations. Following the update, Morningstar raised its fair value estimate to $324 per share, suggesting shares remain attractively priced.

Other top contributors within the Moat Index during the month include global prestige beauty market leader, Estee Lauder (EL), security products and solutions company, Allegion (ALLE), as well as semiconductor design software provider, Synopsys (SNPS).

Companies detracting the most in July came from a mix of sectors, with industrials accounting for two of the five names on the list: United Parcel Service (UPS) and fluidics equipment manufacturer IDEX Corp. (IEX). Other notable detractors included fixed-income trading platform MarketAxess (MKTX), software and analytics giant Adobe Inc. (ADBE), and enterprise cloud leader Salesforce Inc. (CRM).

SMID Moat Index July Highlights: Travel Strength and M&A Activity Shine

The SMID Moat Index outpaced both the small-cap and mid-cap benchmarks in July, driven by strong stock selection across several parts of the portfolio. Industrials and consumer discretionary were standout areas, with four of the month’s top five contributors coming from these two sectors.

Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) topped the SMID Moat Index in July, marking its second consecutive month as a leading contributor. Shares rallied 26% as investor confidence grew around the company’s consistent pricing strength, robust travel demand, and expanding onboard spend. Morningstar believes Norwegian remains well positioned to deliver improving returns on invested capital, supported by efficient scale, cost advantages and brand loyalty across its portfolio. The company’s younger fleet and pipeline of new ships provide ongoing pricing power, while cost initiatives and scale benefits continue to support margin expansion. Morningstar maintains its fair value estimate of $31.50 per share, suggesting shares may still have room to run.

Chart Industries (GTLS), a provider of cryogenic equipment and specialty solutions used across LNG, hydrogen, and industrial gas markets, was also a top contributor to SMID Moat Index performance in July, with shares jumping 20% following news that the company had entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired by Baker Hughes. The all-cash deal, valued at $13.6 billion or $210 per share, marked a premium over Chart’s prior trading levels and sent the stock sharply higher. Morningstar views the offer price as aligned with its fair value estimate and sees the acquisition as a positive outcome for Chart shareholders, offering greater certainty than a prior bid from Flowserve. Despite softer second-quarter sales, the company’s aftermarket service business remains a key strength, and Morningstar continues to believe Chart’s specialty products and repair-driven revenue model support its economic moat.

Companies detracting the most in July within the SMID Moat Index included two names from the auto industry, with auto retailers Lithia Motors (LAD) and CarMax (KMX) weighing on performance during the month. Other notable laggards were toy manufacturer Mattel (MAT), health care supplier Baxter International (BAX), and communications provider Charter Communications (CHTR).

data.title: British American Tobacco: After A Double This Stock Is Fully Valued And Ripe For A Pullback (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I wrote a bullish article on British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) in May 2024, and at that time, the stock was trading for just $30 per share and it was yielding 9.5%. My article suggested that the window could be closing for those wanting to lock in a 9.5% yield and that turned out to be true. Since my article was published, this stock has provided stellar total returns of about 106%, while the S&P 500 Index (SPY) has generated a total return of just under 20% in the same time period. At the time I wrote the article, I felt the stock was too undervalued and the yield was so generous, but after these huge gains, I think it is time to lock in the amazing gains and allocate the proceeds to new investment opportunities and even to money market funds. Let's take a closer look at why I think it is time to sell:

The Chart

By looking at the chart below, you might think this is a fast growing tech stock as it has been on a huge bull run for the past year. Most of the gains have come in 2025, and the stock is now overbought and trading well above key support levels. The 50-day moving average is around $49 and the 200-day moving is just about $40. When I look at this chart, I see a stock that looks overly extended and potentially ripe for a meaningful pullback. With the stock now trading above $56, a pullback to the 50-day moving average would represent a decline of about 10%. A more meaningful correction back to the 200-day moving average would represent a decline of around 30%. I view a decline of this magnitude (30%) as less likely, but I do see at least a 10% pullback as very likely.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The estimates provided by Seeking Alpha below show that this stock is now trading for about 12 times earnings. I believe that given the lack of meaningful growth for the tobacco industry, this represents full valuation if not overvaluation for this stock. As you can see from the revenue estimates below, this company is only expected to grow the top line by about 2% in 2026 and around 3% for 2027. This is not the type of growth that I want to see for a stock that is trading at 12 times earnings.

As for the balance sheet, this company has about $48.49 billion in debt, and $6.72 billion in cash. This shows that there is a significant amount of debt and higher debt levels generally increase risk levels for shareholders. Higher debt levels also typically limit what investors will pay for a stock as well, and this is one more factor that leads me to believe that this stock is fully valued, if not overvalued right now.

The Dividend Is No Longer Attractive Enough For Me

British American Tobacco currently offers a quarterly dividend of $0.75 per share, which yields around 5.5%. In my last article, I saw the dividend yield at 9.5% as being a very attractive investment to lock in while investors were so negative and treating this yield as being unsustainable. It turned out the yield was not sustainable, but not because of a dividend cut, but rather because the stock has just about doubled in value. Because of the rapid share price appreciation, this stock is only yielding around 5.5%, and I don't feel this yield is adequate to compensate for some of the potential risk factors that come with this company and the tobacco industry which includes regulatory risks, high debt levels, and very slow growth, just to name a few.

I also believe a yield of around 5.5% is not attractive enough when compared to other income investments. The average money market fund now yields around 4.2% and I see that as a better overall place to park my money, given the risk to reward I would get from British American Tobacco shares which yield just around 1.3% more than a money market fund, but could be prone to a major pullback of 10% or more.

Here Are My Reasons For Selling

1) The yield no longer is attractive compared to other lower risk income investments.

2) This stock appears overbought, and potentially overvalued, as it now trades well above key support levels. I believe this stock is ripe for a major pullback.

3) In contrast to how negatively many investors and analysts were when I wrote my last article, I now see what could be too much bullishness and this could be the sign of a top. A recent article from Seeking Alpha states that some analysts are getting more bullish on this stock, even after the massive run it has had in 2025. Let's remember, this stock has had total returns of over 100% since my last article in 2024, and this company is only growing revenue in the very low single digits.

4) I believe this is an opportune time to sell into strength. People talk about buying opportunities all the time, but we also need to consider selling opportunities and that is what I see now.

Here Is My Potential Strategy Going Forward

I've sold my position, and I am happy to deploy the proceeds into other income investments. Money market funds are still an attractive place to park cash and considering I see a pullback of 10% or more as likely for this stock, I will happily accept a slightly lower yield in exchange for a big reduction in risk. However, I would consider getting back into this stock if there is a pullback of at least 10%, which would take it back to the 50-day moving average. But, I would only buy a partial position at that level which is around $49, and I would increase my buying, if the stock gets closer to the 200-day moving average. I certainly would not be buying this stock at this time.

Risk Factors And Where I Could Be Wrong

After a very weak jobs report for July, and major downward revisions for May and June, it appears that the Fed will probably lower interest rates in September and that could boost dividend stocks. However, I think this news is already priced into the stock, and it could even be a sell on the news event whereby this stock could even decline in September after a rate cut. But I could be wrong, and this stock definitely has momentum, so that could continue and send the shares even higher. Even though I have valuation concerns, I do believe that management is doing a good job and the company has been posting solid results, which could continue, especially if margins can be expanded and price increases continue. If this company is able to drive strong results in terms of profit margins, it could also keep the stock at elevated levels or push it higher.

In Summary

I feel that this stock has topped out for now, and the easy money has probably been made after doubling since my last article. The sentiment for this stock has gone from being negative to what I feel is overly bullish now given the limited growth potential for the tobacco industry and the ongoing regulatory risks. Given all of this, and because of the huge rally, the yield is no longer enough for me. I believe this is a potential top and therefore a selling opportunity. I also believe a better buying opportunity will likely come in the future with a pullback of at least 10%.
